Economic anxiety also looms large. A plurality of voters say they are worse off financially than a year ago, a perception especially common among Democrats and independents. Responses were weighted to reflect Florida’s electorate by gender, education, race, age, party registration and region. Data was collected using text-to-web and email outreach to registered voters and verified panels. What People Are Saying Spencer Kimball, executive director of Emerson College Polling, said: “Byron Donalds is the clear favorite among Republicans to succeed Governor DeSantis. “Donalds leads all age groups and holds majority support among voters over 60, at 54 percent.” He added: “Florida Hispanic voters are split between Donalds and Jolly: 42 percent support Donalds and 41 percent Jolly. “Men break for Donalds by 14 points, 49 percent to 35 percent, while women break for Jolly by 3 points, 43 percent to 40 percent.” What Happens Next Attention now shifts to whether Democrats can consolidate behind a single candidate and cut into the large pool of undecided voters. Fundraising, endorsements and national political headwinds are likely to play an outsized role as the 2026 race begins to take clearer shape.

text_preview: President Donald Trump’s approval rating among lower-income Americans has dropped sharply in recent months, according to
full_text: President Donald Trump’s approval rating among lower-income Americans has dropped sharply in recent months, according to new polling that shows a steep erosion of support within a key part of his coalition. White House spokesperson Davis Ingle told Newsweek in an emailed statement, "The ultimate poll was November 5, 2024, when nearly 80 million Americans overwhelmingly elected President Trump to deliver on his popular and commonsense agenda." Why It Matters Working-class voters have been central to Trump’s political base, but pressures from the Iran war and a volatile economic outlook are reshaping that relationship. Shifts among low-income voters could carry real consequences for Republican prospects in the November midterms. What To Know New CNN polling conducted by SSRS shows a dramatic decline in Trump’s approval rating among Americans earning less than $50,000 a year. In a CNN/SSRS poll conducted from January 9 to January 12, 2026, Trump’s approval among adults in households earning under $50,000 stood at 38 percent, while 60 percent disapproved of the way he was handling his job as president. That produced a net approval rating (those who approve minus those who disapprove) of minus 22 points for this income group. That survey was based on 1,209 adult Americans recruited from a probability-based panel. According to CNN, the margin of sampling error for total respondents was plus or minus 3.1 percentage points at the 95 percent confidence level. Less than three months later, the numbers worsened substantially. A second CNN/SSRS poll conducted from March 26 to March 30, 2026, found Trump’s approval rating among Americans earning under $50,000 had fallen to 29 percent, while disapproval had risen to 70 percent. That resulted in a net approval rating of minus 41 points. The March poll surveyed a random national sample of 1,201 adults. Results for the full sample had a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.2 percentage points. Taken together, the two surveys show a 19-point net approval swing in the wrong direction among lower-income voters over roughly 10 weeks. Approval fell by 9 points, while disapproval increased by 10 points, according to the CNN data. The income group tracked in both surveys includes many voters who have historically been part of Trump’s working-class coalition, particularly in industrial states and rural areas. The CNN polls asked identical questions about whether respondents approved or disapproved of how Trump was handling his job, allowing for a direct comparison over time. Both polls surveyed adult Americans rather than registered or likely voters, using CNN’s standard approach for measuring broad public sentiment. What People Are Saying White House spokesperson Davis Ingle told Newsweek in an emailed statement: "No other President in history has accomplished more for the American people than President Trump, who is working tirelessly to create jobs, cool inflation, increase housing affordability, and more. The President has already made historic progress not only in America but around the world, and this is just the beginning as his agenda continues taking effect." A White House official previously told Newsweek: "Polling shows President Trump's decision to launch Operation Epic Fury has Americans' support—with MAGA and Republicans strongly supporting it. Despite some online commentators with large followings publicly disagreeing with the president's decision—and many legacy media outlets eagerly highlighting their comments to try and sow division—the MAGA base is not wavering one bit." President Donald Trump said last month: "When this is over, oil prices are going to go down very, very rapidly. So is inflation. So is everything else. But frankly, much more important than short-term or even long-term oil prices: You cannot let the most violent, vicious country in the last 50 years have a nuclear weapon."

text_preview: California and Illinois have begun implementing new federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) restriction
full_text: California and Illinois have begun implementing new federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) restrictions that remove benefit eligibility for many humanitarian immigrants. Why It Matters Previously, several categories of lawfully present immigrants, including refugees, asylees, and certain humanitarian entrants such as trafficking survivors, could qualify for benefits if they met income and other requirements. Under the new rules, many of those groups are no longer eligible. SNAP access is now largely limited to U.S. citizens and a smaller subset of noncitizens, primarily lawful permanent residents (green card holders) who meet additional criteria, along with a few specifically protected categories such as some Cuban and Haitian entrants. Undocumented residents have never been eligible for SNAP. What To Know Now, many immigrants who are legally present in the United States but do not fall into those narrower categories are no longer eligible for assistance, even if they had previously qualified. According to Bay Area news outlet KALW California, it is estimated around 72,000 lawfully present immigrants will lose their benefits which local advocates say will drive more families to food banks already strained by demand. The restrictions stem from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed in July 2025, which narrowed SNAP eligibility for many lawfully present noncitizens and is now being implemented in California as of April 1, 2026. California officials say those already enrolled and newly ineligible under the law will keep CalFresh until their next recertification—typically after 12 months—while new applicants in the affected categories will be denied starting April 1. Illinois also set April 1 as its implementation date for the federal changes, according to the Illinois Department of Human Services. According to Nebraska Public Media, several other states began rolling out the federal restrictions at different times in recent months. Minnesota began implementing the rules on March 1. What People Are Saying Ezer Pamintuan, senior policy advocate at the Alameda County Community Food Bank, told KALW: "So what we're going to see immediately is longer lines at pantries, at food banks, at shelters, people with nowhere else to go are going to have to come to us for help, and this is at a time when food banks are already stretched thin with resources." Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins said on the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July 2025: "The One Big Beautiful Bill marks the start of a new golden age for America and American agriculture. This historic piece of legislation makes permanent the largest tax cuts in history. "While expanding programs to support the farmers who feed, fuel, and clothe America, this legislation also tackles the fraud and waste that has run rampant in the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP). The bill holds states accountable for their error rates, strengthens work requirements, and prevents illegal aliens from receiving SNAP." What Happens Next States, if they have not already begun implementing new rules regarding immigrant status, will do so in accordance with federal law.

text_preview: Young voters are abandoning President Donald Trump, with new polling showing his standing among Gen Z collapsing sharply
full_text: Young voters are abandoning President Donald Trump, with new polling showing his standing among Gen Z collapsing sharply in just a matter of weeks. White House spokesperson Davis Ingle told Newsweek in an emailed statement, “The ultimate poll was November 5, 2024, when nearly 80 million Americans overwhelmingly elected President Trump to deliver on his popular and commonsense agenda.” Why It Matters Support among younger voters, a demographic that was key to Trump’s 2024 victory, is critical as the White House heads toward the 2026 midterms under the shadow of a foreign war and economic strain. A deepening generational divide could reshape turnout and campaign strategy in competitive races. What To Know Trump’s approval rating among Americans aged 18 to 34 has deteriorated dramatically since the start of the year, according to two CNN polls conducted by SSRS. In a survey conducted from January 9 to January 12, 2026, 30 percent of adults in that age group said they approved of how Trump was handling his job as president, while 69 percent said they disapproved. That left him with a net approval rating (those who approve minus those who disapprove) of minus 39 among Gen Z voters. The January poll surveyed 1,209 adult Americans recruited from a probability‑based panel. Results for the full sample carried a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points at the 95 percent confidence level. By late March, those numbers had worsened substantially. A follow‑up CNN/SSRS poll conducted from March 26 to March 30 found Trump’s approval among 18‑ to 34‑year‑olds had fallen to just 20 percent, while disapproval increased to 80 percent, putting his net approval rating among young adults at minus 60. That represents a significant 21‑point swing in net approval in the wrong direction since January, marking one of Trump’s sharpest demographic declines over the period. The March survey was based on a random national sample of 1,201 adults and had a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.2 percentage points. The shift suggests not only fading support but hardening opposition among younger voters as the year has progressed. While Trump remains more competitive with older age groups, Gen Z has emerged as one of his weakest constituencies, with opposition now overwhelming approval by an almost four‑to‑one margin. What People Are Saying White House spokesperson Davis Ingle told Newsweek in an emailed statement: "No other President in history has accomplished more for the American people than President Trump, who is working tirelessly to create jobs, cool inflation, increase housing affordability, and more. The President has already made historic progress not only in America but around the world, and this is just the beginning as his agenda continues taking effect." A White House official previously told Newsweek: "Polling shows President Trump's decision to launch Operation Epic Fury has Americans' support—with MAGA and Republicans strongly supporting it. Despite some online commentators with large followings publicly disagreeing with the president's decision—and many legacy media outlets eagerly highlighting their comments to try and sow division—the MAGA base is not wavering one bit." President Donald Trump said last month: "When this is over, oil prices are going to go down very, very rapidly. So is inflation. So is everything else. But frankly, much more important than short-term or even long-term oil prices: You cannot let the most violent, vicious country in the last 50 years have a nuclear weapon." What Happens Next Attention now shifts to whether Gen Z attitudes stabilize or continue to slide as the war abroad, economic conditions at home and the midterm campaign calendar accelerate. With younger voters already deeply skeptical, any major policy turn—or setback—could further define the political terrain heading into November.

text_preview: The No Kings protest group has said that its recent day of action attracted strong participation from Republican and bat
full_text: The No Kings protest group has said that its recent day of action attracted strong participation from Republican and battleground states. The protest group organized a day of protests in multiple areas across the U.S. on March 28 against the Donald Trump administration. Organizers said over 200,000 protesters came out for the Twin Cities event in a statement emailed to Newsweek Saturday. The group has also said that almost half of these events took place in GOP or swing states. In a statement to media, White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson previously said: “The only people who care about these Trump derangement therapy sessions are the reporters who are paid to cover them.” Why It Matters The No Kings movement has emerged as an influential protest group that has organized multiple days of action against President Donald Trump's administration since he returned to the White House in January 2025. Protests taking place in GOP areas suggests that ill-will towards the Trump administration is spreading and could impact the Republican Party in the upcoming midterm elections in November 2026. However, Trump still commands general support from his base. What To Know According to organizers, nearly half of the No Kings events were held in traditionally Republican or battleground states. They said that there were 127 events in Texas, 121 in Florida and 113 in Ohio. Organizers also said that overall, over 3,100 events were registered across all 50 states, with millions expected to have taken part. While most gatherings were reportedly peaceful, authorities reported clashes late in the day outside federal properties in Los Angeles and Portland. Police issued dispersal orders and made arrests after confrontations at sites associated with immigration enforcement and detention. The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) also said that in downtown Los Angeles, “a group of 1,000 rioters surrounded the Roybal Federal Building,” throwing rocks, bottles and cement blocks. “Two officers hit with the concrete blocks are receiving medical care,” it said on Saturday, adding that two arrests had been made. No Kings previously coordinated around 1,800 protests across the U.S. on June 14, the same day as Trump's birthday and the 250th anniversary of the U.S. Army. Meanwhile, in October, at least 2,700 No Kings events took place simultaneously in cities, towns and suburbs in every U.S. state, against Trump’s policies and agenda. Trump has previously called similar protests "a joke" run by "radical left lunatics." The White House dismissed the latest demonstrations as “Trump Derangement Therapy Sessions.” What People Are Saying Eunic Epstein-Ortiz, a No Kings spokesperson previously told Newsweek: "We're focused on our immediate and ongoing nationwide digital organizing effort to support local organizers on the ground in Minneapolis and across the nation, all leading up to the non-violent mass mobilization on March 28, where we anticipate millions in the streets at local No Kings marches, protests, rallies, and other types of events across all corners of this country."

text_preview: The Republican Party is in striking distance of losing a Florida seat that President Donald Trump won by 25 points in th
full_text: The Republican Party is in striking distance of losing a Florida seat that President Donald Trump won by 25 points in the 2024 presidential election, according to a new poll. The survey, conducted by EDGE Communications and MDW Communications, found that the Republicans hold just a four point lead over the Democrats in the 28th congressional district when the candidates' names are removed, on 45 percent to 41 percent. This is within the poll's margin of error of +/- 4.3 percentage points. When candidates' names are included, the poll found that incumbent Republican Congressman Carlos Giménez is leading his potential Democratic challenger, Hector Mujica, by just six percentage points: 46 percent to 40 percent. A total of 14 percent of respondents were still undecided, Florida Politics reported. Newsweek reached out to Giménez and Mujica by email to comment on this story outside of normal business hours. Why It Matters Losing the seat would affect the balance of power in Congress. Traditionally, the party that does not hold the White House tends to do better in midterm elections and the GOP are hoping to protect their slim majority to ensure they can carry out Trump's agenda. It would also be a blow for Trump to lose a seat in his home state and comes at a time in which Democrats have performed well in special elections in Florida. What To Know The poll also found that Mujicia holds a narrow lead over Giménez among third- and no-party voters on 39 percent to 34 percent, with 27 percent still undecided. Giménez won re-election in Florida's 28th congressional district in November 2024 with 64.6 percent of the vote, to Democratic candidate Phil Ehr's 35.4 percent. Trump won Florida in the 2024 presidential election, beating Kamala Harris with 56.1 percent of the vote to 43 percent. He won the 28th congressional district with 62.36 percent of the vote to Harris' 36.96 percent. The poll was conducted on 514 voters in early March and had a margin of error of +/- 4.3 percentage points. EDGE Communications is advising Mujica's campaign. What People Are Saying EDGE Communications founder and President Christian Ulvert told Florida Politics: “This data confirms what we’re seeing across South Florida: voters are frustrated with the cost of living and tired of the same political dysfunction. “The path to victory for Hector Mujica in CD 28 runs through independent voters, who are already breaking away from Republicans and looking for a credible alternative focused on affordability and accountability.” What Happens Next The election takes place on November 3 2026.

text_preview: Voters are turning colder on Vice President JD Vance, with a new CNN poll showing his approval sliding deeper into negat
full_text: Voters are turning colder on Vice President JD Vance, with a new CNN poll showing his approval sliding deeper into negative territory just months into a turbulent election year. Newsweek contacted the White House via email for comment outside of regular working hours. Why It Matters Public frustration is building as the administration faces an overseas war, rising domestic anxiety, and a looming midterm test. Those pressures also land on Vance as a potential 2028 contender now tied closely to the White House record. What To Know JD Vance’s standing with voters has weakened noticeably since the start of the year, according to two national polls conducted by CNN in partnership with SSRS. In a survey conducted from January 9 to January 12, 2026, 41 percent of adults said they approved of how Vance was handling his job as vice president, while 58 percent disapproved. That put him 17 points underwater at the time. That poll surveyed 1,209 adult Americans recruited through a probability-based panel. Results carried a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points at the 95 percent confidence level. By late March, the numbers had moved further in the wrong direction. A second CNN/SSRS poll conducted from March 26 to March 30 found Vance’s approval at 37 percent, with 62 percent disapproving. His net approval rating fell to minus 25, marking an eight-point net drop since January. This poll was based on a random national sample of 1,201 adults and had a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.2 percentage points. The direction of change is striking. Approval slipped four points, while disapproval climbed by the same amount over roughly two and a half months. Net support deteriorated even faster, suggesting not just softness among supporters but consolidation among critics. The March survey landed as the administration grappled with fallout from the conflict with Iran, continued economic unease at home and intensifying scrutiny ahead of the 2026 midterms. As vice president, Vance has been a prominent public defender of White House policy, leaving little daylight between his profile and the broader administration agenda. Speaking alongside the president in the Oval Office on March 17, Vance said the administration was confronting long‑standing challenges others had avoided, calling Iran “a problem that has festered in this country for far too long.” He added that critics were trying to “drive a wedge” inside the White House, but stressed that he and the president had long shared the same red line, saying, “What the president said consistently going back to 2015, and I agreed with him, is that Iran should not have a nuclear weapon,” adding that the military action was taken “under the president’s leadership.” Polling snapshots do not explain why individual voters disapprove, but the trend line shows erosion rather than stability. In early January, Vance was already underwater. By late March, his deficit had widened significantly, placing him closer to the lower end of approval ratings typically seen during politically difficult periods. What Prediction Markets and Betting Odds Say The polling slide also coincides with Vance’s chances of securing the GOP nominee to run for president in 2028 falling from 53 percent six months ago to 37 percent as of March 31, according to a leading prediction market cited on CNN. Traditional betting markets still place JD Vance among the front-runners for the 2028 presidential race, though the picture is increasingly competitive. Odds from Star Sports list Vance at 4/1, behind only Gavin Newsom at 7/2 overall, with Marco Rubio close at 7/1, suggesting bookmakers still see Vance as a leading contender despite recent political headwinds. On the Democratic side, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez is priced at 7/1 overall and as short as 15/8 within her party market, ahead of figures such as Josh Shapiro, Kamala Harris and Gretchen Whitmer, while longer odds for names like Tucker Carlson, Jon Ossoff and Michelle Obama reflect more speculative scenarios rather than firm expectations. What People Are Saying Vice President JD Vance told USA Today in August last year: “Yes, terrible tragedies happen, but I feel very confident the president of the United States is in good shape, is going to serve out the remainder of his term and do great things for the American people. “And if, God forbid, there's a terrible tragedy, I can't think of better on-the-job training than what I've gotten over the last 200 days.” Speaking about Vance’s chances at securing the 2028 Republican nominee, CNN’s chief data analyst Harry Enten said: “Six months ago, he was at a 53 percent chance. Down he goes. He goes down to 37 percent, according to the Kalshi prediction market.” What Happens Next Attention now turns to whether these numbers harden or rebound as the year unfolds. Upcoming congressional battles, foreign policy developments and the pace of economic news will shape how closely voters continue to associate Vance with outcomes they like—or dislike—well before the 2028 conversation fully begins.

text_preview: In American politics, there is a rule as old as the republic itself: The incumbent president's party will likely lose gr
full_text: In American politics, there is a rule as old as the republic itself: The incumbent president's party will likely lose ground in the midterms. Even popular presidents have not avoided it. It happened under Barack Obama. It happened under Bill Clinton. It happened under Ronald Reagan. After regaining the White House in 2024, President Donald Trump declared victory. His approval numbers climbed. Republicans controlled Congress. The opposition was fractured and adrift. For a season, Trump looked invincible. But the midterms are still seven months away, and in that time, everything has shifted. The gains have evaporated. Republicans are now bracing for what comes next—a reckoning that suggests the old rule will hold once more. The Polling Picture Trump's approval rating sits at 41.3 percent, with 56.3 percent disapproval, according to RealClearPolitics averages. More troubling for Republicans, polling now suggests they could lose not just the House but the Senate as well—a scenario that seemed implausible just months ago. About 39 House seats are deemed competitive. As of today, Democrats need a net gain of only three to regain control of the House. On the Senate side, states once written off as safely Republican—like Texas, Iowa and Ohio—are now in play. Prediction markets reflect the shift. Kalshi, one of the largest prediction markets, prices a Democratic flip of the House at 84 percent. For the Senate, Republicans lead at 51 percent to retain the upper chamber, though Democrats have recently yet briefly surpassed the 50 percent threshold. "I've never seen the fundamentals of an election cycle as bad for an incumbent party as I am right now," said Mike Madrid, a Republican strategist with 35 years in the business. The core problem is the economy. Trump promised 2026 would be a bumper year for growth. Instead, the year has kicked off with job losses, rising gasoline prices and deepening uncertainty. The unemployment rate has climbed to 4.7 percent from 4.4 percent over the past 12 months. Prices at the pump have jumped 19 percent to a national average of $3.45, according to AAA. "The public's anxiety about the economy, specifically their own financial situation, has led to a downward trend in polls for the president," Matt Klink, veteran Republican strategist, told Newsweek. The political damage is severe. Trump's approval rating on inflation has reached an all-time low of -34 points, down from +6 at the beginning of his second term. More Americans now blame Republicans than Democrats for rising inflation—a dramatic reversal from 2024. The Base Cracks The fracturing extends beyond economics. The files related to sex offender Jeffrey Epstein became a flashpoint within the Republican base. Trump had campaigned on releasing them to expose Democratic corruption. But when he took office, he resisted. It took a bipartisan congressional push—a discharge petition, a 427-1 House vote and Senate unanimity—to force his hand. In November 2025, Trump signed the Epstein Files Transparency Act. The releases began in December, heavily redacted. When investigators reviewed the January files, they found the Department of Justice had selectively withheld materials related to accusations against the president. Immigration, once Trump's signature issue, is slipping. After dominating the political agenda for years, it fell behind civil rights and civil liberties concerns in late January, according to YouGov and The Economist tracking—to its weakest standing since Trump took office. The shift coincided with the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, two Minneapolis residents fatally shot by federal immigration agents during enforcement operations. Then came the Iran war, a foreign policy adventure that contradicts the "America First" theology animating Trump's rise. Marjorie Taylor Greene has left Congress. Thomas Massey has become openly confrontational with the White House. Rand Paul speaks publicly against the war. Thom Tillis acknowledges the political liability of gas prices. Eight Republican senators held firm on the government shutdown over Department of Homeland Security funding. The list of Republicans willing to break with Trump—once a handful—has stretched into double digits. For a decade, Republicans treated Trump as an asset to be protected at any cost. That calculation has shifted. In competitive races across the country, Democrats have been overperforming. In special elections throughout 2025, Democratic candidates won by larger margins than former Vice President Kamala Harris did in 2024. In Virginia, James Walkinshaw won by about 50 points. In Rhode Island, Stefano Famiglietti won by 67 points. In Oklahoma, Amanda Clinton won by 69 points, compared to Harris' 19-point victory. The pattern repeated across Pennsylvania, Kentucky, Arizona, Tennessee and elsewhere. Overall, Democrats won or overperformed in 41 out of 42 key elections in 2025, according to the Democratic National Committee. This Is Trump's Loss, Not a Democratic Win The political reversal is not primarily a story of Democratic strength. It is Republican collapse under Trump's watch. Democrats remain deeply unpopular—their net favorability stands at -20 points, according to polls. Generic ballot leads mean little when neither party inspires confidence. What is happening instead is simpler: Voters are punishing Trump. They are rejecting his economic record, his immigration enforcement tactics and his foreign entanglements. They are walking away because he promised one thing and delivered another. Even Republican strategists acknowledge the limits of recovery. Alex Patton, GOP strategist, was blunt: "Realistic path? Never say never, but at this point highly unlikely." To turn things around, he said, Republicans would need nearly everything to change. "Conservatives would need to recapture enthusiasm. Independents would need for trust to be reestablished. Inflation would need to decrease, wars and invasions would need to end, the Epstein files would need to be released, gas prices would need to decrease. I'm not sure there's enough runway to correct these trends," Patton said. Klink also told Newsweek that the problem runs deep. "President Trump and the GOP's problems are more significant than messaging," he said. "The GOP faces the momentum that all midterm politicians face—particularly in second presidential terms. Couple that with incredibly tight minorities in both the House and Senate, and you have the makings of a dissatisfied electorate." Historically, the party controlling the White House loses an average of 22 House seats in the midterms. But Senate retention, once considered secure for Republicans, has become uncertain. "Voters' concerns about affordability, along with rising gas prices, higher healthcare costs, and uncertainty surrounding the Iran War, have made a Democratic Senate takeover more likely," Klink said. But the numbers tell the story voters already sense. Peter Loge, director of George Washington University's Project on Ethics in Political Communication, explains how Americans use elections. "Elections are ways to fire the people in charge if voters don't like what's going on. Economic news has been bad all year and is getting worse. That's bad news for Republicans because Republicans are in charge." What would reverse the trajectory? Nearly everything, according to Klink. The Iran war would need to end swiftly and favorably. Fuel prices would need to plunge to prewar levels. The economy would need to bounce back strongly, and affordability concerns would need to be addressed. "There's still time for Donald Trump and the Republicans, but with seven months until Election Day, the sands are running out," Klink said.

full_text: Iran's military has said that Tehran will deliver "crushing, broader and destructive" attacks on targets linked to the U.S. and Israel, after President Donald Trump threatened to bomb the Islamic Republic "back to the stone age." Iran's military spokesman said that only "insignificant" targets had been struck so far by the U.S. and Israel, which "must pay for the aggression you initiated," according to a statement shared by Iranian state media. The comments follow Trump's televised speech on Wednesday, in which he said that the U.S. would soon complete all of its military objectives against Iran and hit the country extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. It comes as world leaders were meeting Thursday to discuss how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. What To Know - President Donald Trump said in a speech on Wednesday that the U.S. will "finish the job" in Iran in a matter of weeks. - Iran hit back at Trump, warning of attacks on U.S. targets which would be “crushing, broader and destructive." - Oil prices climbed, and stocks fell in response to Trump's speech. As soon as the president stopped speaking, the price of Brent Crude rose five percent to $106 a barrel. - The United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia intercepted a new wave of Iranian drones on Thursday. - French President Emmanuel Macron said Trump's plan to open the Strait of Hormuz is "unrealistic." - Americans have been urged by the U.S. embassy in Baghdad to leave the country. Follow Newsweek for the latest on the war in Iran. In a polarized era, the center is dismissed as bland. At Newsweek, ours is different: The Courageous Center—it's not "both sides," it's sharp, challenging and alive with ideas. We follow facts, not factions. If that sounds like the kind of journalism you want to see thrive, we need you. When you become a Newsweek Member, you support a mission to keep the center strong and vibrant. Members enjoy: Ad-free browsing, exclusive content and editor conversations. Help keep the center courageous. Join today. Apr 02, 2026 at 11:48 AM EDT Polls show Americans unimpressed after president’s speech, key questions unanswered Early polling conducted after President Donald Trump’s national address suggests the speech failed to satisfy Americans looking for clarity on the country’s most pressing concerns, particularly soaring fuel costs and the widening conflict with Iran. The dominant takeaway among voters on Thursday morning was the absence of any concrete plan to ease high gasoline prices at home or abroad. Instead, Trump framed the economic strain as an “investment” in future generations — a message that appears unlikely to blunt public frustration. The speech also revived scrutiny of the administration’s stated war aims. At the outset of the conflict, Trump pledged to dismantle Iran’s missile industry and neutralize its regional proxy forces. Poll respondents noted that neither objective has been achieved more than a month into the fighting. Apr 02, 2026 at 11:35 AM EDT U.S. offers $3 million for tips on attacks in Iraq as embassy warns of new threats The U.S. State Department’s Rewards for Justice program announced Thursday that it is offering up to $3 million for information on recent attacks targeting American diplomatic sites in Iraq, including the U.S. Embassy in Baghdad, the Consulate General in Erbil, and the Baghdad Diplomatic Support Center. “If you have information on these attacks, send us your tip,” the program said on X, urging the public to come forward. The reward notice was issued shortly before the U.S. Embassy in Baghdad warned that Iranian‑aligned Iraqi militias may be planning attacks in central Baghdad within the next 24 to 48 hours. The early‑morning alert urged Americans to leave the country, citing a heightened risk of kidnappings by terrorist groups. The warning followed the abduction of American journalist Shelly Kittleson, who was kidnapped in Baghdad on Tuesday, according to two sources familiar with the case and an Iraqi official. Alex Plitsas, her designated U.S. point of contact and a CNN national security analyst, said the U.S. government had previously warned Kittleson of a specific threat from the Iranian‑backed militia Kata'ib Hezbollah, which was allegedly seeking to kidnap or kill female journalists. Later Thursday, Dylan Johnson, assistant secretary of state for global public affairs, said on X that an individual detained by Iraqi authorities in connection with Kittleson’s abduction had ties to Kata'ib Hezbollah. Apr 02, 2026 at 11:22 AM EDT UAE, Jordan intercept dozens of Iranian missiles and drones after Trump remarks The United Arab Emirates said its air‑defense systems intercepted 19 Iranian missiles and 26 drones on Thursday, a new barrage that came less than 24 hours after President Donald Trump again claimed Iran’s strike capacity had been “dramatically curtailed” by a month of U.S.-Israeli attacks. The Jordanian Armed Forces reported intercepting an Iranian missile as well, saying the projectile was destroyed before reaching populated areas. Both the UAE and Jordan said all of the latest launches were successfully intercepted and that no new casualties were reported from falling debris. The announcements underscored the continued pace of Iranian attacks despite U.S. assertions that Tehran’s missile and drone infrastructure has been severely degraded. Apr 02, 2026 at 11:11 AM EDT Pro-Iran Telegram channel threatens strikes on Israeli transport network A message circulating Thursday on a public Telegram channel aligned with pro‑Iranian groups warned that Israel’s transportation system could face retaliatory attacks following what it called the “American‑Zionist” strike on Karaj’s B1 bridge. The post claimed that Israel’s “Achilles’ heel” lies in several key logistical chokepoints and outlined potential targets, including the Yarkon Bridge, the Jezerel Tunnel, and major highways connecting Tel Aviv, Jerusalem, and Haifa. It also referenced the Port of Haifa and Rehovot Station as strategic hubs that, if disrupted, could hinder military movement. The message further alleged that a land corridor linking Jebel Ali to Ashkelon—used as an alternative supply route amid disruptions at sea—was vulnerable to precision strikes. Apr 02, 2026 at 11:09 AM EDT WATCH: Australian PM calls for ‘greater clarity’ on US goals in Iran war Apr 02, 2026 at 10:56 AM EDT Iranian state TV shows guards writing anti-Trump messages on missiles Iranian state television on Thursday aired footage of members of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps writing political messages on missiles prepared for launch, escalating Tehran’s propaganda campaign as the war continues. In the video, Guards personnel are seen inscribing slogans denouncing President Donald Trump and his administration. One message read, in part: “Thank you to all those who, even in America itself, condemn the war waged by the criminal gang in the White House.” Another referenced Jeffrey Epstein, echoing themes Iranian officials have increasingly used to frame U.S. leadership as corrupt and illegitimate. Apr 02, 2026 at 10:43 AM EDT Major Iranian steel producers halt operations after US-Israeli strikes Two of Iran’s largest steel producers say their facilities have been forced offline following a wave of U.S.-Israeli attacks that targeted industrial infrastructure across the country. The Mobarakeh Steel Company in Isfahan province announced that its “production lines have completely shut down” due to what it described as the high intensity of recent strikes. The company is one of the Middle East’s biggest steelmakers and a critical supplier for Iran’s construction and manufacturing sectors. In Ahvaz, the Khuzestan Steel Company reported similar disruptions. Mehran Pakbin, the firm’s deputy head of operations, told the state‑affiliated Mizan Online outlet that several units have been halted and are unlikely to restart for at least six months. Apr 02, 2026 at 10:36 AM EDT Guterres urges immediate end to war, calls on Iran to reopen Hormuz UN Secretary‑General António Guterres on Thursday issued one of his strongest appeals yet for an immediate halt to the Middle East war, warning that the conflict is inflicting “immense human suffering” and triggering severe economic fallout. Speaking to reporters at the United Nations, Guterres directed his message at all sides. “To the United States and Israel, it is high time to stop the war that is inflicting immense human suffering and already triggering devastating economic consequences,” he said. He also urged Iran to cease attacks on neighboring states “and to open the Strait of Hormuz,” stressing that freedom of navigation must be respected under international law. Apr 02, 2026 at 10:30 AM EDT Prominent Iranian rights lawyer Nasrin Sotoudeh detained without explanation Nasrin Sotoudeh, one of Iran’s most renowned human rights lawyers, has been detained by authorities without any public explanation, her daughter said Thursday. In a post on Instagram, Mehraveh Khandan said her mother was taken into custody on Wednesday night. She added that security forces also seized electronic devices during the raid, offering no details about the charges or the agency responsible for the arrest. Sotoudeh, a longtime defender of political prisoners, women’s rights activists, and dissidents, received the European Union’s Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought in 2012. Her past clients include Nobel Peace Prize laureate Shirin Ebadi, further cementing her status as one of the country’s most high‑profile legal advocates. Apr 02, 2026 at 10:17 AM EDT EU’s Kallas presses China on Hormuz crisis, global economic risks EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas spoke with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi about the mounting economic fallout from the near‑shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz, warning that Iranian attacks on commercial vessels have pushed global shipping to the brink. Kallas said on X that the waterway’s paralysis has become a direct threat to the global economy and energy security. “Iranian attacks on civilian ships, and the threat of more, have brought traffic in the Strait of Hormuz to a near halt,” she wrote. Restoring “safe, toll‑free freedom of navigation,” she added, is now an urgent priority under international maritime law. The two officials also discussed broader EU‑China relations, as Brussels seeks to keep Beijing engaged on crisis‑management efforts while navigating a tense geopolitical landscape. Apr 02, 2026 at 10:04 AM EDT IRGC commander mocks U.S. threats as ‘Hollywood illusions’ A senior Iranian military commander on Thursday dismissed threats from U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and President Donald Trump, calling their warnings against Iran “Hollywood illusions” and insisting that any attempt to destroy the country would lead to more American casualties. Chief Brigadier General Seyed Majid Moosavi, who heads the Aerospace Force of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, reposted Hegseth’s message from X in which the Pentagon chief echoed Trump’s vow to bomb Iran “back to the Stone Ages” if it refuses to end the 33‑day war. Moosavi ridiculed the rhetoric, saying American leaders were threatening “a civilization over 6,000 years old” despite what he described as the United States’ “meager 250‑year history.” Moosavi warned that Washington’s posture would only endanger its own forces. “It is you who are taking your soldiers to the grave, not Iran, who you want to return to the Stone Age,” he wrote. Apr 02, 2026 at 09:51 AM EDT Cooper says global unity growing as 40 nations discuss reopening Hormuz Diplomats from more than 40 countries convened Thursday to explore political and diplomatic steps to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as U.K. Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper said the talks underscored “the strength of our international determination” to restore access to the vital oil corridor. Speaking to officials at the virtual meeting, Cooper accused Iran of effectively “hijacking an international shipping route to hold the global economy hostage,” warning that the prolonged closure has deepened global energy instability. While Thursday’s session focused on non‑military measures, Cooper said defense planners from several nations will also meet separately to map out post‑war security needs. Those discussions are expected to include potential mine‑clearing operations and broader “reassurance” efforts for commercial vessels once fighting subsides. The United States did not participate in the meeting, following President Donald Trump’s repeated assertions that safeguarding the waterway is not America’s responsibility. Apr 02, 2026 at 09:39 AM EDT Iran arrests five in Zanjan for alleged links to ‘hostile’ networks Authorities in Iran say they have detained five people in the north‑western province of Zanjan, accusing them of working with what officials described as “hostile” foreign networks. According to a statement carried by the semi‑official Tasnim news agency, a security unit of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps in Zanjan alleged the individuals were “affiliated with and acting as mercenaries for the enemy.” The group was accused of engaging with networks Tehran links to foreign intelligence services. The arrests come as Iranian officials continue to warn citizens—both inside the country and abroad—against cooperating with organizations tied to U.S. or Israeli interests during the ongoing war. Authorities have repeatedly framed such activity as a threat to national security, part of a broader crackdown that has intensified since the conflict began. Apr 02, 2026 at 09:23 AM EDT Iran issues defiant response after Trump threatens to ‘bring them back to the stone ages’ Senior Iranian military and political leaders delivered a series of defiant statements on Thursday, pushing back against President Donald Trump’s televised claim that the United States and Israel were close to destroying Iran’s military capabilities. In his prime‑time address Wednesday night, Trump said the joint air campaign had eliminated much of Iran’s missile infrastructure and production capacity, boasting that U.S. forces were “on track” to achieve all objectives soon. He warned that strikes would intensify in the coming weeks, saying the assault could drive Iran “back to the Stone Ages,” even as he noted that negotiations to end the conflict were underway. Iranian officials dismissed those assertions. A spokesman for the Revolutionary Guards said in a video message that U.S. assessments of Iran’s military strength were “incomplete,” warning that any belief Tehran’s drone, electronic‑warfare, or specialized weapons programs had been destroyed would only “deepen the swamp” for Washington and its allies. Foreign ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei said on state television that talks with the United States were impossible under escalating bombardment. “We will not tolerate this vicious cycle of war, negotiations, cease-fire and repetition,” he said, arguing that the pattern had destabilized the region for years. Other commanders were even more blunt. Sayed Majid Moosavi, who leads the Guards’ air force, accused the United States of underestimating Iran’s resilience and history. Responding to a post by U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, Moosavi wrote that it was Washington—not Tehran—sending its soldiers “to their graves,” adding that American leaders were deluded if they believed they could threaten “a civilization over 6,000 years old.” Apr 02, 2026 at 09:10 AM EDT Iran says US-Israeli strike damaged major health Research Institute One of Iran’s most prominent medical research centers was hit in the latest round of US‑Israeli strikes, a senior government official said Thursday, prompting renewed warnings from relief agencies about escalating attacks on health facilities across the region. The Pasteur Institute of Iran—described by officials as one of the “century‑old pillars” of public health in the Middle East—sustained damage in what authorities called a direct blow to global health security. Hossein Kermanpour, who heads the information center at Iran’s Ministry of Health, said on X that the strike amounted to “a direct assault on international health security.” Founded in 1920, the institute is Iran’s oldest public health center and a key site for vaccine development, infectious‑disease research, and epidemiological surveillance. Its scientists have played a central role in national vaccination campaigns and regional disease‑prevention efforts for decades. Apr 02, 2026 at 08:52 AM EDT Macron says Trump’s “unrealistic” over opening Strait of Hormuz The French president Emmanuel Macron has said that a military operation to reopen the Strait of Hormuz was “unrealistic” as he took aim at President Donald Trump. Speaking in South Korea, Macron said that a military plan to open the waterway blocked by Iran as Trump has signaled could happen, would not work because it would take a long time and “would expose anyone crossing the strait to coastal threats from the (Iranian) Revolutionary Guards.” Macron said that reopening the strait can only be done in concert with Iran and so required a ceasefire and a resumption of negotiations. The French president also took a swipe at Trump’s handling of the war, saying “when you want to be serious, you don't say the opposite every day of what you said the day before,” adding “perhaps you shouldn't talk every day.” Apr 02, 2026 at 08:42 AM EDT Iran media reports death of senior commander A commander of a special unit of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) was killed in strikes on Wednesday, according to Iranian media. They said Mohammad Ali Fathalizadeh, commander of the Fatehin special unit, was among those killed in the strike, although there were no further details given.. The announcement on Thursday comes on a day in which Iranian media also reported how U.S. and Israeli strikes hit several points near Azimiyeh in Karaj, west of Tehran, including what was described as the B1 bridge, injuring several people. . Apr 02, 2026 at 08:30 AM EDT Oil forecast to cost $113 a barrel in Q2: analysis Despite President Donald Trump’s televised remarks signaling further attacks on Iran, analysis firm Oxford Economics has not changed its baseline forecast for Brent crude oil prices, which it says will average $113 per barrel in the second quarter of this year. In a statement to Newsweek, the firm said that Trump’s comments which gave a two-to-three-week timeframe for the war although did not clarify an exit strategy, had not significantly altered the balance of risks. Oxford Economics chief global economist Ryan Sweet said that the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively closed, and the baseline assumes that it won’t change until the end of April, removing additional oil supply from the market and adding to the economic costs with each passing day. Tankers passing through the strait are 98 percent below what was seen the week before the war and Oxford Economics expects traffic to increase to about 50 percent in May and June. This would help ease but not alleviate the oil shortfall of around two (million barrels per day (mbpd) that is poised to grow. This will also add to the upward pressure on oil prices this quarter and early next. “We estimate that the average Q2 oil supply disruption will be around 7.5mbpd, but if the military timeline is extended, risks are that oil prices will rise more than we currently anticipate, and that would both extend and increase the economic costs,” said Sweet. Apr 02, 2026 at 08:00 AM EDT Iranian commander hits back at Trump’s “Stone Age” comments” A senior Iranian commander has responded to comments by President Trump warning of bombing Iran “back to the Stone Age." An account attributed to Majid Mousavi of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) responded to comments by US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth following President Donald Trump’s speech. “It is you who will take your soldiers under gravestones, not Iran that you can return to the Stone Age,” said a post on the account attributed to Majid Mousavi of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps IRGC). The post added the U.S. was threatening “a civilization thousands of years old.” A spokesman for Iran’s military Lt. Col. Ebrahim Zolfaghari said earlier that Iran still had hidden stockpiles of arms, munitions and production facilities and that the centers the U.S. had targeted “are insignificant.” “Our strategic military productions take place in locations of which you have no knowledge and will never reach,” Zolfaghari added. Apr 02, 2026 at 07:30 AM EDT Americans in Iraq told to leave country “now” The U.S. embassy in Baghdad has urged Americans to leave Iraq in a message which warned of the threat posed by “Iran-aligned militias.” In a statement on X, the American mission said that there could be attacks in central Baghdad within 24-48 hours. "The Iraqi government has not prevented terrorist attacks in or from Iraqi territory. Iran-aligned terrorist militia groups may claim to be associated with the Iraqi government. Terrorists may carry identification denoting their status as Iraqi government employees." “These actors may target U.S. citizens, companies, universities, diplomatic facilities, energy infrastructure, hotels, airports, and other sites believed to be associated with the United States” the statement said. Iraqi institutions and civilian targets were also at risk according to the embassy which said consular services were suspended and U.S. citizens were urged to “leave Iraq now.” Apr 02, 2026 at 07:00 AM EDT Strait of Hormuz closure hurts “a raft of industries”: analyst The closure of the Strait of Hormuz will have knock-on effects for many industries, a financial expert has told Newsweek. Susannah Streeter, chief investment strategist, Wealth Club said that there are “fresh frissons of fear about the duration of the war with Iran” after President Donald Trump’s televised speech on Wednesday which gave no indication the conflict was very close to ending, and signaled more intensive attacks. Streeter told Newsweek the big concern will be about further damage to energy facilities across the Gulf. “The repair work is already likely to take years, and further destruction is likely to keep oil and gas prices elevated for even longer. “ Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz which has tightened the supply of billions of dollars’ worth of petrochemical products which usually flow through the Strait. “Prices of plastics and polymers have headed to highs not seen for four years, and this is already having an impact on the price and availability of household goods,” Streeter said. Cleaning products manufacturer McBride, which makes Oven Pride, has warned that its costs are rising and it is seeing the first signs of supply shortages due to the war in Iran. “This experience could be the precursor to problems to come for swathes of businesses reliant on the packaging industry,” added Streeter. Apr 02, 2026 at 06:30 AM EDT Oil prices could climb higher: analyst The price of a barrel of Brent crude which spiked on Thursday after President Donald Trump warned of further strikes on Iran, could go even higher, according to one analyst. On Thursday, the price of the international benchmark jumped 7.4 percent to $108.69 per barrel while benchmark U.S. crude rose 7.1 percent to $107.24 a barrel after Trump’s remarks in a televised address. Nikos Tzabouras, senior market analyst at Tradu, told Newsweek that Trump’s comments doused hopes for an imminent resolution and kept the geopolitical risk premium elevated. “Prices could climb even higher as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut, oil infrastructure in the region is damaged and output is being curtailed as storage fills up,” Tzabouras said Thursday. “The longer these disruptions continue, the tighter the market becomes, raising the risk that previously unfavorable fundamentals are overtaken by events.” Apr 02, 2026 at 06:00 AM EDT Iran says Pasteur Institute was hit, posing health threat An Iranian health ministry official has said the Pasteur Institute of Iran had been struck in an attack which posed a health security risk., Hossein Kermanpour said in an X post that the institute was “a century-old pillar of global health” and that an attack on was “a direct assault on international health security.” The post showed images of the building in rubble. His claims have not been independently confirmed but they come amid further strikes against Iranian targets. The Israeli military said Thursday that it struck an Islamic Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) ground forces base and a mobile command post during attacks across Tehran. It said the strikes on Wednesday, also hit a ballistic missile storage site in Tabriz and that the IDF had identified that Iranian commanders were relocating command centers into mobile units. Apr 02, 2026 at 05:15 AM EDT Iran’s army says no troops in ground invasion “should come out alive” Iran’s army chief Abdolrahim Hatami has warned no troops in a land invasion of Iran would come out alive. In comments on Thursday, Hatami ordered operational headquarters to monitor the prospect of a ground invasion and prepare to counter assault. Enemy movements and actions must be tracked and response plans Put in place, he said. “If the enemy launches a ground operation, not a single one of them should come out alive,” Hatami said, according to Iran International, an independent news outlet. The Trump administration has not rule out sending in troops on the ground into Iran, amid a big military buildup in the region. Apr 02, 2026 at 04:45 AM EDT Rubio highlights Trump’s four Iran War objectives U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio has boiled down President Donald Trump’s speech about the Iran War to four key points. In a post on X following Trump’s televised address, Rubio wrote that the U.S. president had “delivered a powerful speech” and was “clear about our objectives in Iran." Using bullet points, Rubio said these were to destroy Iran’s weapons factories, navy, air force and chances of ever having a nuclear weapon. ”The President’s leadership sends a message to the world that the United States will defend its people and its interests, and uphold peace through strength,” the post added. Read More: Donald Trump’s Four Objectives For Ending Iran War Revealed Apr 02, 2026 at 04:15 AM EDT Iran’s president asks whether Trump is putting “America First” Iran’s president Masoud Pezeshkian has written an open letter asking whether the Trump administration’s war with his country was serving America’s interests. In a message posted on social media addressed “to the people of the United States” Pezeshkian referred to President Donald Trump’s prior rhetoric in writing “Is ‘America First’ truly among the priorities of the US government today?” He said that Iran has “never, in its modern history, chosen the path of aggression, expansion, colonialism, or domination” and that the perception of his country being a threat is “the product of political and economic whims of the powerful.” He said that Iran’s drone and missile strikes on civilian and military infrastructure across the Gulf have been “legitimate self-defense.” It was not clear whether other parts of Iran’s leadership were involved in drafting the letter. Apr 02, 2026 at 03:40 AM EDT Iran warns of “crushing” attacks on U.S. and Israeli targets Iran has responded with a defiant message following President Donald Trump’s warning that U.S. attacks would continue for at least another couple of weeks. Trump said in a televised address Wednesday that the U.S. would continue with its attacks on Iran over the next two to three weeks and boasted of destroying Iran’s military and navy. However, on Thursday, the spokesperson for the Iranian military cited by state media warned of “crushing, broader and destructive" attacks on U.S. and Israeli targets. The Iranian spokesman added that the U.S. and Israel have "incomplete" information about Tehran's military capabilities and equipment and rejected Trump’s claim that Iran’s nuclear stock had been depleted. Meanwhile, Ali Akbar Velayati, a senior adviser to Iran’s supreme leader said Thursday the Strait of Hormuz would remain open to global shipping but “closed to the enemies of the Iranian people and their bases in the region.”

text_preview: President Donald Trump has outlined the United States' four main objectives for the Iran War in his first national addre
full_text: President Donald Trump has outlined the United States' four main objectives for the Iran War in his first national address since the conflict began at the end of February. After the speech had concluded, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio summarized the goals in a post on X as "destroy their weapons factories, destroy their navy, destroy their air force" and "destroy their chances of ever having a nuclear weapon." Rubio added: "The president’s leadership sends a message to the world that the United States will defend its people and its interests, and uphold peace through strength." Rubio's post followed Trump's speech on Wednesday night that lasted just under 20 minutes, in which the president said U.S. forces would "finish the job" soon and "core strategic objectives are nearing completion". He pledged to hit Iran "extremely hard over the next two to three weeks" and bring them "back to the Stone Ages". Trump also said the United States' objectives are "very simple and clear" and the military was "systematically dismantling the regime’s ability to threaten America or reject power outside of their borders." He added: "That means eliminating Iran’s navy, which is now absolutely destroyed, hurting their air force and their missile program at levels never seen before and annihilating their defense industrial base. We’ve done all of it. Their navy is gone. Their air force is gone. Their missiles are just about used up or beaten. "Taken together, these actions will cripple Iran’s military, crush their ability to support terrorist proxies and deny them the ability to build a nuclear bomb," added Trump. During his speech, Trump reiterated that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon and said the Islamic Republic was building a vast arsenal of ballistic missiles that were a threat to the U.S. However, he did not mention the possibility of sending U.S. ground troops into Iran amid a large military build-up in the region. The president also did not refer to any progress in negotiations with Iran, despite his previous insistence that Tehran wanted to do a deal. Nor did he bring up his April 6 deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz that Iran has effectively blocked, causing energy market turmoil. Global markets fell on Thursday after Trump’s speech gave no clear sign of when the war would end, with oil prices climbing six percent Thursday to over $107 a barrel for Brent crude. They had fallen earlier this week amid hopes of de-escalation. Trump also did not refer to NATO whose members has criticized for what he says is a reluctance to help the U.S. war effort. An interview published earlier in the day saw him tease the prospect of withdrawing the U.S. from the alliance. In a polarized era, the center is dismissed as bland. At Newsweek, ours is different: The Courageous Center—it's not "both sides," it's sharp, challenging and alive with ideas. We follow facts, not factions. If that sounds like the kind of journalism you want to see thrive, we need you. When you become a Newsweek Member, you support a mission to keep the center strong and vibrant. Members enjoy: Ad-free browsing, exclusive content and editor conversations. Help keep the center courageous. Join today.

text_preview: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has announced that Department of Homeland Security (DHS) staff affected by the ongoing
full_text: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has announced that Department of Homeland Security (DHS) staff affected by the ongoing partial shutdown will receive an extension on the nationwide federal tax filing deadline. Tax season ends this month, with returns and payments to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) due on April 15. Why It Matters DHS has been shut down since February 14. On Wednesday, Republican leaders in Congress agreed to move forward with legislation to fund most of the agency's operations, excluding those tied to immigration enforcement. The shutdown caused hourslong security delays at some airports last month, as Transportation Security Administration (TSA) staff, which is part of DHS, either resigned or failed to report to work after enduring weeks without pay. Conditions began to improve earlier this week when President Donald Trump signed an executive order to restore paychecks for TSA employees. The filing extension offers further relief for DHS employees who went unpaid during the partial government shutdown. What To Know "The ongoing DHS shutdown under the radical left has created unnecessary disruptions, putting an unfair burden on DHS personnel and their families," Bessent wrote on X on Wednesday. "As they continue to show up under extraordinary circumstances without receiving a paycheck, President Donald Trump, the U.S. Treasury, and the IRS will provide affected DHS employees with a 30-day automatic extension for this tax filing season with penalty and interest relief," he continued. With the extension, the deadline for tax returns for DHS staff is May 15. On April 1, congressional Republicans unveiled an agreement, saying the House of Representatives would move forward with a previously approved bipartisan Senate bill to fund DHS, excluding Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol. They also indicated that separate legislation would be expedited to provide longer-term funding for those agencies. Although ICE and Border Patrol are not included in the immediate funding measure, both divisions have already received significant financial support through last year's One Big Beautiful Bill Act. What People Are Saying Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a news release: "We are committed to supporting our hard-working DHS officers and employees so they can stay focused on their mission and keep the American people safe without being penalized for missing a tax filing deadline." House Speaker Mike Johnson and Senate Majority Leader John Thune said in a joint statement: "In the coming days, Republicans in the Senate and House will be following through on the President's directive by fully funding the entire Department of Homeland Security on two parallel tracks: through the appropriations process and through the reconciliation process." What Happens Next For most other filers, unless a personal tax extension is granted, the deadline for filing and payments remains April 15.

text_preview: President Donald Trump praised the U.S. economy during his prime-time speech on Wednesday night about the war on Iran, a
full_text: President Donald Trump praised the U.S. economy during his prime-time speech on Wednesday night about the war on Iran, as polls show a bleaker reality facing him. Why It Matters At home, Americans faced the sharpest jump in gasoline prices since 2022—passing a $4 national average—deepening political stakes for the Trump administration and shaping expectations for the address. Shifts in approval and disapproval ratings could also signal broader erosion and complicate the Republican Party's midterm strategies, especially as multiple trackers show Trump significantly underwater nationally. Recent surveys recorded record or near‑record lows within key demographics, including men and independent voters, potentially compounding headwinds for the White House amid public concern over the cost of living in the U.S. and the ongoing war with Iran. What To Know During his address to the nation Wednesday, the president said in part, "We built the strongest economy in history. We're going through it right now, the strongest in history, in one year, we've taken a dead and crippled country. I hate to say that, but we were a dead and crippled country after the last administration and made it the hottest country anywhere in the world by far with no inflation. Record-setting investments coming into the United States, over $18 trillion. And the highest stock market ever, with 53 all-time record highs in just one year." "It all positioned us to get rid of a cancer that has long simmered. It's known as the nuclear Iran," Trump said. Amid the backdrop of Trump's speech, however, looms a new reality for the president: a plunge in his approval ratings. Trump hit a new approval-rating low for his second term in office, a survey from CNN conducted by SSRS shows on Wednesday. According to the poll, Trump's approval rating is 35 percent, with a disapproval rating of 64 percent. The survey was taken from March 26 to March 30 among 1,201 respondents, with a 3.2 percent margin of error. CNN notes that Republicans who strongly approve of Trump's job performance dropped from 52 percent in January to 43 percent in the poll out on Wednesday. Trump's approval rating for handling the economy has also dropped to a new low of 31 percent, the poll shows. In a poll from The Economist and YouGov released on Tuesday, the president's approval rating with Republicans was 81 percent and his disapproval rating was 14 percent. The survey polled 1,679 U.S. adults from March 27 to March 30, with a 3.2 percent margin of error. The last time the president's approval rating dipped to 81 percent or lower among Republicans was in a poll from January 16 to January 19, when it hit 79 percent with the group, surveys from the pollster show. Trump's approval rating on Iran week over week shows an 8 percent drop, the poll from The Economist and YouGov also shows. In the poll on Tuesday, 68 percent of Republicans approve of the job the president is doing versus 22 who disapprove. Last week, 76 percent of Republicans approved and 14 percent disapproved. What People Are Saying Former Georgia Republican Congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene, on X following Trump's speech: "I wanted so much for President Trump to put America First. That’s what I believed he would do. All I heard from his speech tonight was WAR WAR WAR. Nothing to lower the cost of living for Americans. Nothing to reduce our near $40 trillion in debt. Nothing to save Social Security, which goes bankrupt in just a few years. Nothing to lower the cost of insurance. Nothing to address jobs for Americans. Nothing about education for our children. Nothing about our children’s future. Nothing for America’s future. I’m so beyond done. I pray for our military and their families. I pray for innocent people all over the world. I pray for peace and prosperity for all." Secretary of State Marco Rubio, also on X Wednesday: "President Trump delivered a powerful speech tonight. He was clear about our objectives in Iran: • Destroy their weapons factories • Destroy their navy • Destroy their air force • Destroy their chances of ever having a nuclear weapon The President’s leadership sends a message to the world that the United States will defend its people and its interests, and uphold peace through strength."

text_preview: Austria became the latest country to effectively close its airspace to U.S. and Israeli forces headed for Iran on Thursd
full_text: Austria became the latest country to effectively close its airspace to U.S. and Israeli forces headed for Iran on Thursday, joining at least four other countries in Europe, amid ongoing tensions between Washington and its allies over the war. The traditionally neutral nation cited this stance when confirming the news to Austrian broadcaster ORF. While officials were not issuing an outright ban, requests from the United States were being dealt with on a case-by-case basis. Newsweek reached out to the Pentagon via email for comment. European Nations Pushing Back Against the US The refusal follows steps similar to those taken by Spain and Italy, which have also restricted U.S. military access to airspace or bases during the conflict—moves that have drawn criticism from President Donald Trump and the White House and raised questions about alliance cohesion. Spain’s decision last week to bar U.S. military aircraft from its airspace was seen as a particularly sharp break given its NATO membership. Earlier this week, Trump publicly criticized France after it declined to allow aircraft carrying U.S. military supplies to Israel to transit French airspace. Trump accused Paris of being “very unhelpful” and warned that the United States would “remember” the decision, framing it as part of a broader lack of European support for the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran. French officials later pushed back, saying France had not imposed a blanket closure but was reviewing military overflight requests individually in line with its long‑standing policy and international law. Switzerland, citing its neutrality laws, rejected most of the U.S. requests to use its airspace during the Iran war, according to the news agency Keystone-SDA, via Swiss Info. Of the 11 applications it received from the U.S. between March 5 and 23, only four were approved by the Federal Office of Civil Aviation, and one was withdrawn, the report stated. What People Are Saying A spokesperson for Austria's Defense Ministry told Newsweek: "For reasons of neutrality, Austria refuses overflights and the transit of troops if these would serve to provide military support to a party to any conflicts. If it is known that a military aircraft or military vehicle is directly or indirectly involved in a conflict, overflight or transit will be denied in consultation with the ministry of foreign affairs." President Donald Trump, in a televised address on Wednesday evening: "Thanks to the progress we've made, I can say tonight that we are on track to complete all of America's military objectives shortly, very shortly. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We're going to bring them back to the Stone Ages where they belong." United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres, speaking Thursday: “To the United States and Israel, it is high time to stop the war that is inflicting immense human suffering and already triggering devastating economic consequences." What Happens Next World leaders are meeting on Thursday to discuss reopening the Strait of Hormuz, as the conflict continues.

text_preview: Austria has denied the United States permission to use its airspace for military operations related to the war with Iran
full_text: Austria has denied the United States permission to use its airspace for military operations related to the war with Iran, citing the country’s long‑standing policy of neutrality. A spokesperson for Austria's defense ministry told Newsweek on Thursday: "For reasons of neutrality, Austria refuses overflights and the transit of troops if these would serve to provide military support to a party to any conflicts. If it is known that a military aircraft or military vehicle is directly or indirectly involved in a conflict, overflight or transit will be denied in consultation with the ministry of foreign affairs." The move places Austria among a growing number of European countries restricting U.S. military access as Washington and Israel continue their campaign against Iran. Why It Matters Austria’s decision underscores mounting diplomatic friction between the U.S. and several European partners as the Middle East conflict deepens. While Austria is not a NATO member, its central geographic position in Europe makes its airspace strategically significant for military transit between Western Europe and the Middle East. The refusal follows steps similar to those taken by Spain and Italy, which have also restricted U.S. military access to airspace or bases during the conflict—moves that have drawn criticism from the White House and raised questions about alliance cohesion. Spain’s decision last week to bar U.S. military aircraft from its airspace was seen as a particularly sharp break given its NATO membership. Austria’s action further expands the list of European states limiting cooperation, reinforcing a broader pattern of hesitation among U.S. allies over involvement—direct or indirect—in the war with Iran. What To Know According to ORF Radio, Austria’s Defense Ministry confirmed that it has rejected U.S. requests to use Austrian airspace for military operations connected to the conflict, invoking national neutrality laws enshrined after World War II. A ministry spokesperson said there had been several requests from Washington but did not specify how many. Each request was refused because Austrian law prohibits providing military support to any party engaged in an active armed conflict, the spokesperson said. Austria’s move comes as tensions have escalated between Washington and other European governments over airspace access. Earlier this week, U.S. President Donald Trump publicly criticized France after it declined to allow aircraft carrying U.S. military supplies to Israel to transit French airspace. Trump accused Paris of being “very unhelpful” and warned that the United States would “remember” the decision, framing it as part of a broader lack of European support for the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran. French officials later pushed back, saying France had not imposed a blanket closure but was reviewing military overflight requests individually in line with its long‑standing policy and international law. Austria’s move comes as airspace access across Europe and the Middle East has become increasingly contested amid the escalating conflict, forcing military planners to seek longer, more complex transit routes for flights. Is Austria in NATO? Austria is not a member of NATO and has maintained a constitutionally enshrined policy of military neutrality since 1955, a status adopted after the Allied occupation following World War II ended. While Austria cooperates with NATO on limited security matters and participates in some European Union defense initiatives, it is legally barred from joining military alliances or providing direct military support to countries engaged in active conflicts. That neutrality was central to the government’s decision to deny U.S. military overflight requests linked to the war with Iran. Austrian officials emphasized that the refusal does not amount to a blanket ban on all U.S. military flights through Austrian airspace but applies specifically to operations directly connected to active combat, in line with Austria’s neutrality law. As a non‑NATO country surrounded largely by alliance members, Austria’s stance reflects both its legal obligations and a broader effort to avoid entanglement in a widening conflict that has already strained relations between Washington and several European governments. What Happens Next It remains unclear whether the United States will formally respond to Austria’s decision or seek alternative transit routes through other European countries. U.S. officials have previously criticized European governments for what they describe as a lack of solidarity during the conflict. Update 4/2/26, 11:30 a.m. ET: This article was updated with additional information.

text_preview: The Philippines has reached an agreement with Iran on safe passage for its vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, a move
full_text: The Philippines has reached an agreement with Iran on safe passage for its vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, a move the United States treaty ally says is vital to its energy supply. In a phone call on Thursday, Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi and Philippine Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro reached a "positive understanding on the safety of our seafarers and the security of our energy supply,” Lazaro said on X. "Grateful for the warm spirit of cooperation." Newsweek reached out to the U.S. State Department and the Philippine Department of Foreign Affairs for comment.
